frameworks.name: population receptive field (pRF) mapping
frameworks.one_line: Voxel-based pRF estimates that conflate many neurons with scattered RFs

frameworks.name: spatial frequency selectivity to checkerboard patterns
frameworks.one_line: Uses SF tuning to back out average single-neuron RF size noninvasively

frameworks.name: cross-species fMRI-electrophysiology validation
frameworks.one_line: Matches noninvasive estimates against invasive single-unit recordings in NHP

core_question: Can fMRI estimate average single-neuron visual receptive field sizes noninvasively in humans?
key_assumptions: Spatial frequency selectivity to checkerboards reflects underlying single-neuron RF size
Voxel pRF reflects a population with scattered individual RFs rather than a single neuron
NHP single-unit physiology is a valid ground truth for the new fMRI estimate
manipulations: Compared new SF-based estimate to prior pRF estimates
Compared human fMRI to nonhuman primate fMRI and electrophysiology
key_findings: Obtained noninvasive average single-neuron RF estimates across human early visual cortex
Single-neuron RF estimates were significantly smaller than prior pRF estimates
fMRI and electrophysiology in nonhuman primates showed exceptionally good match
interpretations: Spatial-frequency selectivity provides a valid noninvasive proxy for average single-neuron RF size
Conventional pRF overestimates single-neuron RF because it pools across scattered RFs
